file_name: NETGEAR,INC_04_21_2003-EX-10.16- AMENDMENT #2 TO THE DISTRIBUTION AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Section 2, TERRITORY, is amended to be United States, [*].
perturbation.changed_text: Section 2, TERRITORY, is amended to include most of the United States, unless otherwise determined.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'most of the United States, unless otherwise determined' introduces ambiguity regarding the exact territory covered. This could lead to disputes about which states are included and gives NETGEAR an undefined level of power to exclude territories.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-306 implies good faith and reasonableness in determining requirements in exclusive dealings. The ambiguous 'most of the United States, unless otherwise determined' lacks these standards, potentially allowing Netgear to arbitrarily reduce the territory, thereby acting in bad faith and unreasonably diminishing the Distributor's requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith , except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by including "unless otherwise determined," introduces an ambiguous element that undermines the requirement of good faith and reasonableness in determining requirements, as implied by UCC § 2-306. The original contract clearly defined the territory, whereas the changed contract allows for arbitrary exclusion of territories, potentially acting in bad faith and unreasonably diminishing the Distributor's requirements. This directly contradicts the principle that requirements should be determined in 'good faith,' as specified in Scraped Law Snippet 1.

perturbation.original_text: The undersigned has read this Amendment, agrees hereto, and is an authorized representative of its respective party.
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned has generally reviewed this Amendment, agrees in principle hereto, and represents they are acting in a capacity they believe to be authorized.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'has read this Amendment' to 'has generally reviewed this Amendment' weakens the assurance that the representative fully understands and agrees to the contract terms. 'Agrees in principle hereto' suggests a lack of full agreement, and 'acting in a capacity they believe to be authorized' casts doubt on the representative's actual authority to bind their company. This could lead to enforceability issues.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code § 313 pertains to the authority of corporate officers to execute documents. By changing the language to suggest uncertainty about the representative's authority ('acting in a capacity they believe to be authorized'), the contract introduces ambiguity that can undermine the legal presumption of proper authorization under California law, making enforcement more difficult if authority is challenged.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CORP 313 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Corporations Code - CORP TITLE 1. CORPORATIONS [100 - 14707] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1947, Ch. 1038. ) DIVISION 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW [100 - 2319] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) CHAPTER 3. Directors and Management [300 - 318] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) 313. Subject to the provisions of subdivision (a) of Section 208, any note, mortgage, evidence of indebtedness, contract, share certificate, initial transaction statement or written statement, conveyance, or other instrument in writing, and any assignment or endorsement thereof, executed or entered into between any corporation and any other person, when signed by the chairperson of the board, the president or any vice president and the secretary, any assistant secretary, the chief financial officer or any assistant treasurer of such corporation, is not invalidated as to the corporation by any lack of authority of the signing officers in the absence of actual knowledge on the part of the other person that the signing officers had no authority to execute the same. (Amended by Stats. 2015, Ch. 98, Sec. 6. (SB 351) Effective January 1, 2016.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the representative's authority, which can undermine the legal presumption of proper authorization under California Corporations Code § 313. The original text states the representative 'is an authorized representative,' while the changed text states they are 'acting in a capacity they believe to be authorized.' This weakens the assurance of actual authority, potentially conflicting with the legal standard outlined in the provided snippet from California Code, CORP 313, which protects transactions unless the other party has 'actual knowledge' of the signing officers' lack of authority. By introducing doubt about the representative's actual authority, the changed text creates a scenario where enforcement becomes more difficult if authority is challenged, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Amendment shall remain in effect for the current term and any renewal term of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment may remain in effect for the current term and any renewal term of the Agreement, subject to potential modifications.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall remain' to 'may remain, subject to potential modifications,' the contract introduces uncertainty regarding the duration and stability of the amendment. The possibility of 'potential modifications' without specifying the process or conditions under which they can occur creates ambiguity that can be exploited.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1698 specifies how written contracts can be modified. By stating 'subject to potential modifications' without outlining how these modifications must occur, the contract introduces ambiguity that could conflict with the requirements of § 1698, such as needing a written agreement or executed oral agreement supported by new consideration, making it difficult to determine if changes were legally made.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1698 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. EXTINCTION OF CONTRACTS [1682 - 1701] ( Title 5 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Modification and Cancellation [1697 - 1701] ( Heading of Chapter 3 amended by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109. ) 1698. (a) A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing. (b) A contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement to the extent that the oral agreement is executed by the parties. (c) Unless the contract otherwise expressly provides, a contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement supported by new consideration. The statute of frauds (Section 1624) is required to be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (d) Nothing in this section precludes in an appropriate case the application of rules of law concerning estoppel, oral novation and substitution of a new agreement, rescission of a written contract by an oral agreement, waiver of a provision of a written contract, or oral independent collateral contracts. (Repealed and added by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract language 'shall remain' indicates a definitive obligation regarding the amendment's effect. The changed text weakens this obligation by using 'may remain' and adding 'subject to potential modifications.' This introduces ambiguity about the amendment's duration and allows for changes without specifying a process, potentially conflicting with California Civil Code § 1698, which outlines how written contracts can be modified. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 1698 (a), (b), and (c) detail that written contract modifications need to be in writing or meet specific conditions (executed oral agreement or new consideration), implying a structured modification process. The changed text's ambiguity undermines this structured approach, creating a contradiction.
